meta.source: NHS Fit for Travel / WHO (manual check)
meta.disclaimer: General guidance only. Consult your GP or travel clinic ideally 6–8 weeks before departure. Some vaccinations require multiple doses over several weeks.

AR.name: Argentina

malaria.notes: No malaria risk in Argentina.

AR.notes: Ensure routine UK vaccinations are up to date (MMR, tetanus, diphtheria, polio). No specific requirements for tourists visiting Buenos Aires or Patagonia.

AU.name: Australia

malaria.notes: No malaria risk.

AU.notes: Ensure routine UK vaccinations are up to date. No specific requirements or recommendations for standard tourist visits.

BR.name: Brazil
recommended: Yellow Fever (if visiting Amazon or Pantanal)

malaria.areas: Amazon region and Pantanal only. No risk in Rio, São Paulo, Curitiba, or other major cities.
malaria.notes: Antimalarial medication recommended if visiting Amazon or Pantanal. Speak to your GP about which type.

BR.notes: Yellow fever vaccination certificate required if arriving from certain African or South American countries with yellow fever. Recommended (not required) if visiting Amazon/Pantanal areas. Not needed for standard tourist visits to Rio, São Paulo, or Curitiba.

CA.name: Canada

malaria.notes: No malaria risk.

CA.notes: Ensure routine UK vaccinations are up to date. No specific requirements for tourists.

CO.name: Colombia

malaria.areas: Some rural areas below 1,600m, including parts of the Amazon and Pacific coast. No risk in Bogotá, Medellín, or Cartagena.
malaria.notes: Prophylaxis recommended if visiting rural or jungle areas outside major cities.

CO.notes: Yellow fever certificate required if arriving from certain countries. Yellow fever vaccination recommended if visiting jungle areas east of the Andes.

CU.name: Cuba

malaria.notes: No malaria risk.

CU.notes: Ensure routine UK vaccinations are up to date. No specific requirements for tourists.

EG.name: Egypt

malaria.areas: Negligible risk in El Faiyum governorate (June–October only). No risk in tourist areas.
malaria.notes: Antimalarials not recommended for standard tourist visits.

EG.notes: Ensure routine UK vaccinations are up to date. Hepatitis A, B, and typhoid recommended for most tourists. Travellers' diarrhoea is common — take precautions with food and water.

ID.name: Indonesia

malaria.areas: Risk in some outer islands (Kalimantan, Lombok, Flores, Sumba, Papua). Minimal risk in Bali and Java.
malaria.notes: No prophylaxis needed for standard Bali visit. Discuss with GP if visiting outer islands or rural areas.

ID.notes: Rabies is present in Bali — important to be aware. If you are scratched or bitten by an animal seek medical attention immediately, as rabies is fatal without prompt treatment. Avoid street dogs.

IN.name: India

malaria.areas: Risk across most of India year-round, higher in rural areas and during monsoon season. Lower risk in major cities.
malaria.notes: Antimalarial medication recommended for most visits outside major cities. Discuss options with your GP.

IN.notes: One of the highest vaccination-recommendation destinations. Book a travel clinic appointment 6–8 weeks before travel. Yellow fever certificate required if arriving from an endemic country.

JP.name: Japan

malaria.notes: No malaria risk.

JP.notes: Ensure routine UK vaccinations are up to date. No specific recommendations for standard tourist visits. Tick-borne encephalitis vaccination may be considered for rural hiking in some areas.

KE.name: Kenya
required: Yellow Fever (if arriving from endemic country)

malaria.areas: High risk across most of Kenya year-round, including Nairobi and Mombasa.
malaria.notes: Antimalarial medication strongly recommended. Use insect repellent (DEET-based) and sleep under a mosquito net.

KE.notes: Kenya has one of the higher vaccination requirements of popular destinations. Yellow fever vaccination is strongly recommended regardless of entry requirement. Ensure all routine vaccinations are current.

KR.name: South Korea

malaria.notes: No meaningful malaria risk for tourists.

KR.notes: Routine UK vaccinations should be up to date. No significant health risks for standard tourist visits.

LK.name: Sri Lanka

malaria.areas: Malaria effectively eliminated in Sri Lanka.
malaria.notes: No prophylaxis needed.

LK.notes: Rabies present — avoid contact with animals. Japanese Encephalitis vaccination considered for long stays or rural visits.

MA.name: Morocco

malaria.notes: No malaria risk.

MA.notes: Routine vaccinations should be current. Travellers' diarrhoea risk — drink bottled water and be cautious with street food.

MX.name: Mexico

malaria.areas: Very low risk in some rural areas of Pacific coast and southern states. No risk in Mexico City, Cancún, or main tourist areas.
malaria.notes: Prophylaxis not needed for standard tourist visits to major destinations.

MX.notes: Ensure routine vaccinations are current. Food and water hygiene important in rural areas.

MY.name: Malaysia

malaria.areas: Risk in jungle and rural areas, particularly Sabah and Sarawak (Borneo). No risk in Kuala Lumpur or Penang.
malaria.notes: Discuss antimalarials with GP if planning jungle trekking in Sabah/Sarawak.

MY.notes: Rabies present in some areas — avoid contact with animals.

NZ.name: New Zealand

malaria.notes: No malaria risk.

NZ.notes: Ensure routine UK vaccinations are current. No specific recommendations for standard tourist visits.

PE.name: Peru

malaria.areas: Amazon lowlands (including Iquitos area). No risk in Lima, Cusco, or Machu Picchu (high altitude).
malaria.notes: Prophylaxis recommended if visiting Amazon. Not needed for Machu Picchu or Lima visits.

PE.notes: Yellow fever vaccination recommended before visiting Amazon areas.

PH.name: Philippines

malaria.areas: Risk in rural Palawan and parts of Mindanao. No risk in Manila, Cebu, Boracay, or most tourist islands.
malaria.notes: Prophylaxis generally not needed for standard tourist visits. Consider if visiting remote parts of Palawan.

PH.notes: Rabies present — if bitten or scratched by an animal seek medical attention immediately.

QA.name: Qatar

malaria.notes: No malaria risk.

QA.notes: No significant health risks for standard tourist visits. Routine UK vaccinations should be current.

SG.name: Singapore

malaria.notes: No malaria risk.

SG.notes: Excellent healthcare standards. No specific health risks for tourists. Routine UK vaccinations should be current.

TH.name: Thailand
recommended: Rabies (if rural or working with animals)

malaria.areas: Risk in forested border areas with Myanmar and Cambodia. No risk in Bangkok, Phuket, Chiang Mai, or Koh Samui.
malaria.notes: Prophylaxis generally not needed for standard tourist visits to main destinations.

TH.notes: Most tourists visiting Bangkok and popular beach areas need only standard precautions. Discuss Japanese Encephalitis with your GP if planning extended rural travel.

TR.name: Turkey

malaria.areas: Malaria effectively eliminated. Historic very low risk in far southeast (Cukurova area) not relevant for tourists.
malaria.notes: No prophylaxis needed.

TR.notes: No significant health risks for standard tourist visits. Routine vaccinations should be current.

TZ.name: Tanzania

malaria.areas: High risk across mainland Tanzania and Zanzibar year-round.
malaria.notes: Antimalarials essential. Use insect repellent (DEET) and sleep under a mosquito net.

TZ.notes: Yellow fever certificate required if arriving from certain countries; vaccination strongly recommended regardless. Pack insect repellent and malaria medication.

AE.name: United Arab Emirates

malaria.notes: No malaria risk.

AE.notes: No specific health requirements for tourists. Routine UK vaccinations should be current.

US.name: United States

malaria.notes: No meaningful malaria risk for tourists.

US.notes: Ensure routine UK vaccinations are up to date. No specific travel health requirements.

VN.name: Vietnam

malaria.areas: Some risk in rural highland areas in the south. No meaningful risk in Hanoi, Ho Chi Minh City, Hoi An, Ha Long Bay, or major tourist areas.
malaria.notes: Prophylaxis generally not needed for standard tourist visits to main destinations.

VN.notes: Most tourists visiting cities and popular sites need only standard precautions. Consider Japanese Encephalitis vaccine for extended rural stays.

ZA.name: South Africa

malaria.areas: Risk in Limpopo, Mpumalanga (Kruger Park area), and northern KwaZulu-Natal. No risk in Cape Town, Johannesburg, or Garden Route.
malaria.notes: Prophylaxis recommended if visiting Kruger National Park or these provinces. Not needed for Cape Town or Johannesburg.

ZA.notes: Yellow fever certificate required if arriving from an endemic country. Rabies present — avoid contact with animals.
